title: Akbarן¿½s Bridge
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Jelaludin Muhammed Akbar, Guardian of Mankind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Moved his standards out of Delhi to Jaunpore of lower Hind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where a mosque was to be builded, and a lovelier neן¿½er was planned;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Munim Khan, his Viceroy, slid the drawings ן¿½neath his hand.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (High as Hope upsheered her towers to the promised Heavens above.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Deep as Faith and dark as Judgment her unplumbed foundations dove.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wide as Mercy, white as moonlight, stretched her fore courts to the dawn;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Akbar gave commandment, ן¿½Let it rise as it is drawn.ן¿½)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then he wearied, the mood moving, of the men and things he ruled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he walked beside the Goomti while the flaming sunset cooled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Simply, without mark or ensign, singly, without guard or guide,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he heard an angry woman screeching by the riverside.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas the Widow of the Potter, a virago feared and known,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In haste to cross the ferry, but the ferry-man had gone.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So she cursed him and his office, and hearing Akbarן¿½s tread,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (She was very old and darkling) turned her wrath upon his head.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he answered, being Akbar, ן¿½Suffer me to scull you oן¿½er.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Called her ן¿½Mother,ן¿½ stowed her bundles, worked the clumsy scow from shore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till they grounded on a sand-bank, and the Widow loosed her mind;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the stars stole out and chuckled at the Guardian of Mankind.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Oh, most impotent of bunglers! Oh, my daughterן¿½s daughterן¿½s brood,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Waiting hungry on the threshold for I cannot bring their food,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till a fool has learned his business at their virtuous grandamן¿½s cost,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And a greater fool, our Viceroy, trifles while her name is lost!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Munim Khan, that Sire of Asses, sees me daily come and go
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As it suits a drunken boatman, or this ox who cannot row.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Munim Khan, the Owlן¿½s Own Uncle, Munim Khan, the Caponן¿½s seed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Must build a mosque to Allah when a bridge is all we need!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Eighty years I eat oppression and extortion and delays,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Snake and crocodile and fever, flood and drouth, beset my ways.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Munim Khan must tax us for his mosque whateן¿½er befall;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Allah knowing (May He hear me!) that a bridge would save us all!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While she stormed that other laboured and, when they touched the shore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Laughing brought her on his shoulder to her hovelן¿½s very door.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But his mirth renewed her anger, for she thought he mocked the weak;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So she scored him with her talons, drawing blood on either cheek. . . .

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Jelaludin Muhammed Akbar, Guardian of Mankind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Spoke with Munim Khan his Viceroy, ere the midnight stars declined,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Girt and sworded, robed and jewelled, but, on either cheek appeared
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Four shameless scratches running from the turban to the beard.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Allah burn all Pottersן¿½ Widows! Yet, since this same night was young,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One has shown me by sure token, there was wisdom on her tongue.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yes, I ferried her for hire. Yes,ן¿½ he pointed, ן¿½I was paid.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he told the tale rehearsing all the Widow did and said.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he ended, ן¿½Sire of Asses, Capon, Owlן¿½s Own Uncle, know
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I, most impotent of bunglers, I, this ox who cannot row
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I, Jelaludin Muhammed Akbar, Guardian of Mankind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bid thee build the hag her bridge and put our mosque from out thy mind.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So ן¿½twas built, and Allah blessed it; and, through earthquake, flood, and sword,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still the bridge his Viceroy builded throws her arch oן¿½er Akbarן¿½s Ford!
meaning: About: Joseph Rudyard Kipling (December 30, 1865 ן¿½ January 18, 1936) was an English author and poet, born in Bombay, India, and best known for his works The Jungle Book (1894), The Second Jungle Book (1895), Just So Stories (1902), and Puck of Pook's Hill (1906); his novel, Kim (1901); his poems, including Mandalay (1890), Gunga Din (1890), and "Ifן¿½" (1910); and his many short stories, including "The Man Who Would Be King" (1888) and the collections Life's Handicap (1891), The Day's Work (1898), and Plain Tales from the Hills (1888). He is regarded as a major "innovator in the art of the short story"; his children's books are enduring classics of children's literature; and his best work speaks to a versatile and luminous narrative gift.
